# Model Comparison Study - dataBank CI Customer 360

> *[Version française disponible : [model_comparison.md](model_comparison.md)]*

**Auto-generated by `ml/comparison.py` - do not edit by hand.**

- Scenario A: 140 real customers, 35 positives (25.0%)
- Scenario B: 540 real + synthetic customers, 151 positives (28.0%)

## Results (5-fold stratified cross-validation)

| scenario           | model                   |   auc |   recall |   precision |    f1 |
|:-------------------|:-----------------------|------:|---------:|------------:|------:|
| A (n=140 real)      | LogisticRegression     | 0.913 |    0.829 |       0.62  | 0.698 |
| A (n=140 real)      | Rule-Based Scoring     | 0.83  |    0.914 |       0.451 | 0.604 |
| B (n=540 enriched)  | LogisticRegression     | 0.944 |    0.934 |       0.737 | 0.822 |
| B (n=540 enriched)  | RandomForestClassifier | 1     |    0.987 |       1     | 0.993 |
| B (n=540 enriched)  | XGBClassifier          | 1     |    1     |       1     | 1     |

## Reading the results

These metrics remain indicative: see `docs/ml_problem_definition_en.md` for
the limitations of the proxy label used and the sample-size warnings.

**On Scenario B's near-perfect scores (RandomForest, XGBoost):** synthetic
customers are generated through business-rule bootstrapping from real
customers (see `src/synthetic_data_generator.py`), so they stay
statistically very close to their source customer. A high-capacity model
(forest, boosting) can memorize these patterns easily - these high scores
reflect how easy the synthetic dataset is, not a guarantee of production
performance on unseen customers.
